Guide · New Contractors

Construction Software for New Contractors (2026)

Starting a contracting business? Here's what software you actually need — and what to skip until you're bigger.

When you're just starting your contracting business, software decisions can feel overwhelming. Every platform promises to transform your business. Most of them cost real money. And you're already juggling licensing, insurance, marketing, and actually doing the work.

This guide cuts to what you actually need in year one versus what can wait.

Year one priorities: the essentials

1. Estimating and proposals (non-negotiable)

Before anything else, you need a way to produce professional estimates and proposals. Handwritten quotes and Word document estimates lose you jobs — they signal that you're not running a professional operation, even if your actual work is excellent.

Minimum bar: a template in a tool that produces clean, branded estimates with line-item pricing and a clear total. Better: software that lets you send the estimate, collect a digital signature on the contract, and accept a deposit payment online.

2. Invoicing and payment collection

You need to get paid. Chasing paper checks costs time and delays cash flow. Even a basic invoicing tool with online payment (QuickBooks Self-Employed, Houzz Pro, Wave for Freelancers) pays for itself immediately in faster payment and professional presentation.

3. Basic bookkeeping

Track income and expenses from day one. Even if you're not doing job costing yet, you need to know what you're making and spending for tax purposes and business decisions. QuickBooks Simple Start or Wave (free) are both adequate starting points.

Year one: what can wait

  • Complex project management software: If you're running 1–5 projects at a time and you know where everything is, you don't need a $499/month PM platform yet
  • Enterprise scheduling tools: A well-managed shared Google Calendar is enough for most new contractors
  • CRM software: A spreadsheet tracking leads is fine until you have enough volume to justify a dedicated tool
  • Payroll software: If it's just you, handle it yourself. Once you have employees, add payroll software (Gusto is excellent)

The right starting stack

Option A: Houzz Pro ($85/month) — Best for residential contractors

Houzz Pro covers estimating, contracts, invoicing, payment collection, and basic project management in one platform — plus it puts you in front of homeowners looking for contractors. For a new residential contractor, this is the highest-ROI first software purchase.

New residential contractors: Houzz Pro handles estimates, invoices, and leads all in one place.

Try Houzz Pro Free — 30 Days

Option B: JobTread ($99/month) — Best for margin-focused new contractors

If you want to build good job costing habits from the start, JobTread is the right tool. Learning to track actual costs vs. estimates early means you sharpen your estimating faster and understand your business better. The discipline developed in year one with JobTread pays dividends for years.

JobTread builds the job costing habits every contractor needs — start right from day one.

Try JobTread Free

Option C: QuickBooks Simple Start + free estimating tool

The most minimal viable stack: QuickBooks for bookkeeping and invoicing (~$30/month), plus a free or cheap estimating tool. This covers your absolute basics at the lowest cost, but lacks project management, scheduling, and the professional client-facing features of the above options.

When to upgrade to Buildertrend

Buildertrend at $499/month is genuinely the best residential construction management platform. But for a startup, it's typically more than you need and more than you should spend before you have revenue to support it. The typical path:

  • Under $500K revenue: Houzz Pro or JobTread
  • $500K–$1.5M: Either of the above is still often sufficient; evaluate if you're feeling pain points
  • Over $1.5M, 10+ active projects: Buildertrend starts making clear sense
  • Over $3M, complex projects: Buildertrend is probably worth it even at the Complete tier

When you're ready to scale — Buildertrend is where residential contractors grow into:

Explore Buildertrend

Building your tech stack over time

StageRevenueRecommended stack
Launch<$200KQuickBooks + Houzz Pro or JobTread
Growing$200K–$1MHouzz Pro or JobTread + QuickBooks
Scaling$1M–$3MBuildertrend + QuickBooks + Gusto (payroll)
Established$3M+Buildertrend Complete + QuickBooks + dedicated estimating if needed

The habits that matter more than the tools

Honest advice for new contractors: the best software is the one you actually use consistently. A free tool you use every day beats a premium platform you open twice a month. Build the habits first:

  • Send every estimate through software — no more verbal quotes
  • Collect digital signatures on every contract
  • Document every change in scope before doing the work
  • Invoice promptly when work is complete
  • Track your actual costs on every job, even if manually at first

Those habits are what separate contractors who build profitable businesses from those who stay stuck in the feast-or-famine cycle. The software makes those habits easier to maintain — but it doesn't create them.
